question: Tell me about the hardest bug you've shipped a fix for. How did you find the root cause?
expectedPoints: Specific context: stack, scale, who was affected, how it was discovered
What made it hard — intermittent, cross-system, observability gap, etc.
Investigation method (binary search through logs, reproducing locally, narrowing the trigger)
How they validated the fix didn't just paper over the symptom
What changed afterward to prevent the class of bug (test, monitoring, doc, process)
followUps: What signal would have surfaced this earlier?
If you had to do it again, what would you skip?

question: Tell me about a time you disagreed with a product or design decision. How did you handle it?
expectedPoints: Substance of the disagreement — not personal, anchored in user/business impact
How they made their case (data, prototype, user research, trade-off doc)
Willingness to commit and execute even after losing the argument
Whether the outcome validated the position; what they learned either way
Avoidance of villain framing — partner is portrayed as reasonable
followUps: What's a disagreement you lost where the outcome proved you wrong?
How do you decide which battles to push on?

question: Walk me through a project you led end-to-end. What was your role, what shipped, and what didn't go to plan?
expectedPoints: Concrete scope — who, what, when, measurable outcome
Their specific decisions (not 'we did X' — 'I decided X because Y')
Sequencing: how they de-risked, what they cut, what they de-prioritized
Honest 'what didn't go to plan' — not a humble-brag failure
How they multiplied others (review, mentoring, unblocking, delegating)
followUps: What would a 2× version of that project have looked like?
Who on the team grew the most because of how you led it?

question: Tell me about a performance or quality improvement you drove. How did you measure success?
expectedPoints: Baseline metric — what was wrong, how it was quantified, who felt it
Hypothesis-driven approach, not 'I optimized random things'
Specific levers pulled (with trade-offs named)
Result tied to a user-visible or business-visible number
Guardrails added to prevent regression (budget, alert, perf test)
followUps: What did you measure that turned out not to matter?
How would you spot a regression of this win next quarter?

question: Tell me about a technical trade-off you made that you'd defend even though it was unpopular at the time.
expectedPoints: Clear articulation of the alternatives considered
Why the unpopular choice was better given constraints (time, team skill, scale)
Evidence they actually evaluated the alternatives, not just dismissed them
What it cost them socially or politically, and how they handled that
What signal would have changed their mind
followUps: Would you make the same call today with what you now know?
Who do you check this kind of decision with before committing?

question: Tell me about a time you had to ramp up on an unfamiliar technology or codebase quickly to ship something. How did you approach it?
expectedPoints: Concrete situation — deadline, what was unfamiliar, why you couldn't just delegate
Strategy: where they started (docs, tests, git blame, asking specific people)
How they distinguished essential context from nice-to-have
First commit / first PR — what they intentionally kept small
What they captured for the next person in the same spot
followUps: What did you assume early that turned out to be wrong?
When did you ask for help vs. push through?

question: Tell me about a time you helped a teammate level up. What was the gap, and what did you do?
expectedPoints: Specific person, specific gap (not a vague 'mentored juniors')
How they diagnosed the gap before prescribing a fix
What they did vs. what they let the teammate struggle with
How they measured whether it actually stuck
What they learned from the teammate in return
followUps: How do you mentor someone more senior than you on a specific topic?

question: Tell me about a time you pushed back on scope to ship something on time. What did you cut and what did you protect?
expectedPoints: What was at risk if scope didn't move (deadline, quality, team burnout)
Framework for what to cut — user value, reversibility, dependency risk
How they brought stakeholders along instead of just announcing the cut
What got protected and why (the non-negotiables, e.g. a11y, security, core flow)
How they communicated the cut as a v2 plan, not a death
followUps: What's the highest-leverage thing you've ever cut?
What's something you cut that you later regretted?
